ontology.foodex2_description: Tea and tea-based beverages — herbal infusion, ready-to-drink
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX covers herbal infusion RTD beverages; no specific lemongrass-ginger packaged tea code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged bottle of lemongrass ginger tea is approximately 300 ml (300 g), providing about 75 kcal and 18.6 g of carbohydrates per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Lemongrass Tea with Ginger (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for lemongrass tea with ginger (less sugar, packaged), cr_id D05010030. All per-100g values are as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged lemongrass-ginger tea with less sugar. GI estimated at 52 (low) by analogy with lightly sweetened herbal tea / dilute sugar solution beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (52 × 18.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 9.7 g. This is a conservative estimate; actual GI may vary with sugar type and concentration.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (6.0 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold; sugar-sweetened beverages independently raise blood pressure risk (AHA 2018) despite the low sodium content of 3 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this packaged sweetened herbal tea and instead brew unsweetened lemongrass-ginger tea at home, as the added sugar content independently elevates cardiovascular and blood pressure risk regardless of the low sodium level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 6.0 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar raises postprandial blood glucose rapidly and the ADA classifies SSBs as a hard avoidance category for type 2 diabetes management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely, as the 6.0 g/100 g added sugar with no fibre buffer causes rapid glycaemic excursions; unsweetened home-brewed lemongrass-ginger tea is a suitable alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and cardiovascular event risk; this product contains 6.0 g sugar/100 g with no protective fibre, potassium, or antioxidant density in its packaged form.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened packaged herbal tea and choose unsweetened herbal infusions to avoid the adverse lipid and glycaemic effects associated with regular SSB consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Despite the anti-inflammatory potential of lemongrass and ginger, the 6.0 g/100 g added sugar in this packaged formulation exerts a pro-inflammatory effect that negates any herbal benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should brew fresh lemongrass and ginger tea without sugar to obtain genuine anti-inflammatory benefit, as the added sugar in packaged versions counteracts the therapeutic properties of the herbs.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemongrass Tea with Ginger (Less Sugar, Packaged) is a ready-to-drink (RTD) herbal beverage commercially produced in Singapore and widely available across Southeast Asia. It is brewed from lemongrass (Cymbopogon citratus) and ginger (Zingiber officinale), then sweetened with a reduced quantity of sugar before being bottled or canned. The flavour profile is mildly citrusy and gently spicy, with the bright, lemony aroma of lemongrass balanced by the warm, slightly pungent heat of ginger. The texture is thin and watery, similar to a lightly sweetened herbal infusion. Per 100 g, the beverage provides 25 kcal, 6.2 g carbohydrate (of which 6.0 g is sugar), negligible protein and fat, and 3 mg sodium. Despite the 'less sugar' label, the product still contains 6.0 g of added sugar per 100 g, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under both ADA and AHA definitions. This is clinically significant: SSBs are associated with elevated postprandial blood glucose, increased triglycerides, and higher blood pressure risk. Individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease are advised to avoid this product and instead brew unsweetened lemongrass-ginger tea at home. Lemongrass and ginger individually possess documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, but these benefits are substantially diminished in a commercially sweetened packaged format. The beverage is vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-suitable. It is commonly consumed at breakfast or as a refreshment throughout the day in Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: Is lemongrass ginger tea good for diabetes?
How much sugar is in lemongrass tea?
Can I drink lemongrass ginger tea if I have high blood pressure?
Is less sugar herbal tea safe for diabetics?
What is the GI of lemongrass tea?
Calories in packaged lemongrass ginger tea

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010030) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore foods. All per-100g values are exact matches from the HPB entry.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this is a water-based herbal tea beverage containing no protein-bearing ingredients in meaningful quantity. fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this packaged herbal tea contains no fat-bearing ingredients. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — the beverage is a filtered liquid with no fibre-bearing solids retained. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this specific packaged lemongrass-ginger tea are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate based on lightly sweetened herbal tea / dilute sugar solution data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in approved sources. The 'less sugar' designation is a relative marketing claim; the product still qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage under ADA and AHA thresholds at 6.0 g sugar/100 g.
